ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for pizza bun; closest defensible match is bread roll food product (FOODON_03315873). Pizza-specific topping composition not captured in FoodOn.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A000E covers bread and rolls as a category. No specific pizza bun subcategory exists; approximate match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for pizza bun; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard pizza bun weighing approximately 100 g, typically consumed as a snack or light breakfast item.

nutrient_source.title: Pizza Bun — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Pizza Bun (cr_id F05020081). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical entry with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — white bread and refined wheat bread products
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for pizza bun in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 70 based on the closest analogous food: white bread / refined wheat bread roll (GI ~70–72, University of Sydney GI Database). Pizza bun is made primarily from refined wheat flour with modest fat and protein content that slightly moderates glycaemic response. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (70 × 28.41) / 100 = 19.9 per 100 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 517 mg per 100 g with minimal potassium offset
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit pizza bun consumption to half a bun per occasion and avoid pairing with additional salty condiments, as the sodium content of 517 mg per 100 g substantially contributes to daily sodium limits of 2000 mg recommended by the Singapore HPB.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycaemic index (~70) and carbohydrate 28.4 g per 100 g with low dietary fibre 2.65 g
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should avoid or strictly limit pizza bun due to its high GI of approximately 70 and carbohydrate load of 28.4 g per 100 g; if consumed, limit to half a bun and pair with a protein source and non-starchy vegetables to attenuate postprandial glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 9.55 g per 100 g, elevated sodium 517 mg per 100 g, and processed meat (ham) contributing saturated fat and sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume pizza bun sparingly given its combination of elevated sodium, total fat from processed ham and cheese, and refined carbohydrate content, all of which are associated with adverse cardiovascular outcomes when consumed in excess.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Tomato paste (nightshade ingredient), refined wheat flour driving high glycaemic load, and potential trace trans fat from processed ingredients
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid pizza bun due to the presence of tomato paste (a nightshade ingredient that may exacerbate joint inflammation in sensitive individuals), refined wheat flour contributing to high glycaemic load, and potential trace trans fat from processed ham and commercial bread production.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pizza bun is a baked bread roll product popular in Singapore as a snack or breakfast item. It is made from a soft refined wheat flour dough topped or filled with pizza-style ingredients including ham, diced onion, tomato paste, and typically cheese. The bun is baked until the dough is soft and slightly golden, with the toppings melted and caramelised on the surface. The texture is soft and pillowy with a savoury, slightly tangy topping layer. Nutritionally, pizza bun provides 239 kcal per 100 g, with 9.75 g protein, 9.55 g fat, and 28.41 g carbohydrate. Sodium is notably high at 517 mg per 100 g, driven by ham, tomato paste, and cheese. Dietary fibre is modest at 2.65 g per 100 g. Calcium is 116 mg per 100 g, contributed by cheese. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 70 (high), making this food unsuitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes or metabolic syndrome without careful portion control. The high sodium content raises concern for individuals with hypertension. Pizza bun is not vegetarian due to ham content and is not halal-certified in most commercial preparations. It is commonly sold in Singapore bakeries and convenience stores as a grab-and-go breakfast or snack.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a pizza bun?
Is pizza bun high in sodium?
What is the GI of pizza bun?
Is pizza bun suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05020081) with confidence 0.95. GI is an analogous estimate based on refined wheat bread roll from the University of Sydney GI Database, which is the closest approved GI source available. Confidence is high for macronutrients and secondary nutrients; GI is estimated.
notes: Fields set to -1 indicate data not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this entry: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. These fields are set to -1 as the HPB Foodo database entry for Pizza Bun (F05020081) does not report detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel beyond calcium and iron, or moisture content. USDA FoodData Central was consulted as a secondary reference but no exact match for Singapore-style pizza bun was found; the HPB source is the primary and most appropriate source for this Singapore food item. GI of 70 is an analogous estimate based on white bread / refined wheat bread roll from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for pizza bun was found in any approved GI source. Pizza bun is classified as a Western-style food adapted for the Singapore market; no mandatory multilingual names apply under the Veritas multilingual rules (which require Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi only for Singaporean dishes — pizza bun is a Western food category sold in Singapore, not a traditional Singaporean dish). Local name fields are set to null accordingly.
